questions.question: What is the unit of energy?
questions.answer: Joule (J)

questions.question: What is work?
questions.answer: The transfer of energy from one object to another through a force applied over a distance.

questions.question: What is potential energy?
questions.answer: The energy an object has due to its position or configuration in a gravitational field.

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.answer: The energy an object has due to its motion.

questions.question: What is the formula for kinetic energy?
What is the unit of work?
questions.answer: Joule (J)

questions.question: What is power?
questions.answer: The rate at which work is done or energy is transferred.

questions.question: What is the formula for power?
questions.answer: P = W/t

questions.question: What is a watt?
questions.answer: One joule per second.

questions.question: What is the difference between work and energy?
questions.answer: Work is the transfer of energy, while energy is the capacity to do work.

questions.question: What is the law of conservation of energy?
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.answer: Watt (W)

questions.question: What is a kilowatt-hour?
questions.answer: One thousand joules per second for one hour.

questions.question: What is the difference between a kilowatt and a watt?
questions.answer: One kilowatt is one thousand watts.

questions.question: What is the unit of energy transfer?
questions.answer: Joule (J)

questions.question: What is a watt-hour?
questions.answer: One joule per second for one hour.

questions.question: What is the formula for energy transfer?
questions.answer: E = W/t

questions.question: What is a kilowatt-hour used for?
questions.answer: To measure energy consumption.

questions.question: What is the unit of power in the CGS system?
questions.answer: Erg per second.

questions.question: What is the difference between a kilowatt and a watt-hour?
questions.answer: One kilowatt-hour is one thousand times larger than a watt-hour.
